text: 3.2.3. Object detection based Varroa mite detection An object detector approach for Varroa mite detection is shown in the paper (2021), where the authors use CNN-based object detection techniques SSD and YOLO to classify and localize the Varroa mite in a single step. The authors use a custom dataset with bees in various locations to train the networks in order to classify bees and Varroa mites. The best results were obtained in the classification of infected bees using the YOLO object detector. The main advantage of this approach, compared to the previous ones, is the potential of real-time measurement and analysis due to short inference times of the selected algorithm. Nevertheless, a practical evaluation of this experiment has not been performed yet. The authors of (2022) use a similar approach as in (2022), where they use one camera monitoring the brood in the opposite beehive frame and the second one, which captures the tops of the frames. Frame holding the cameras also contains custom electronics, which facilitate sending data through WiFi in the online mode. The whole system is powered by an external battery and a solar cell. The authors use a Fast-RCNN for the individual bee detection and the Hough-transform for the Varroa mite detection with a bee detection accuracy of 88% and a mite detection accuracy of 77%. A disadvantage is that the cameras have to be placed at a much higher distance than is a bee space, which might lead to brood cooling. In the paper (2023) the authors primarily use sound analysis and Mel spectrogram on their own sound dataset to distinguish strong and weak bee colonies. To have a comparison with the other techniques, they use SSD, various YOLO and DETR object detectors together with the dataset (Schurischuster and Kampel, 2020b) containing healthy and Varroa mite infested bees. Using the object detection techniques, authors reached the best mAP score of 0.97 using the YOLOv5s model. The authors of the study Divasón et al. (2023) use the Faster-RCNN object detector with a different backbones to detect Varroa mites on a small custom dataset of a bee brood and Varroa mites. Due to the small resolution of the dataset captured by a smartphone, the authors also used super-resolution techniques to increase the resolution of the input images. The best results with the mAP score of 0.74 were achieved with the pretrained ResNet-50-FPN backbone and the authors conclude, that the super-resolution techniques did not significantly enhanced the model performance. The authors also plan to continue with the experiments with a higher-resolution dataset. In another paper (2023a), the authors use a two-stage detection system, where they first use a segmentation network to

doc_id: 094Machine learning and computer vision techniques in continuous beehive monitoring applications A survey.txt
text: separate the bees followed by an YOLOX model with an enhanced feature extraction called F-YOLOX_b to detect the Varroa mites. The authors created a custom dataset available together with the model at (2023b), where they also use data augmentation techniques (based on the manual extracting and pasting of the Varroa mite) to increase the number of the infested bees. F-YOLOX_b performance is also compared with the standard YOLOX model, YOLOv4 and FasterRCNN, where the proposed model reaches the highest performance in the Varroa mite detection with the mAP score of 0.95. Authors also compare the measured infestation rates with the actual ones.

text: 3.3.3. Object detection based bee traffic monitoring In the paper (2021) the authors propose a system for insect monitoring in their foraging environment. The proposed HyDaT (Hybrid Detection and Tracking) algorithm tracks individual insects offline, one by one in a video sequence for the purpose of recording actions taken by the insects in the foraging environment. YOLO is used as the deep learning image object detector, and a kNN based segmentation for background separation. A ‘‘predict and detect’’ approach is used to associate the detection and the insect of interest. The authors see the major contribution to the state-of-the-art in the application of modern computer vision approaches to pollinator tracking in complex environments, and showing that computationally inexpensive algorithms can be successfully used in detection and tracking tasks allowing low-power devices – such as Raspberry Pi – to run these applications. A complex study (2021) presents a real-time system based on NVIDIA Jetson complemented by a Raspberry Pi 3 based environmental sensing module, which aims to detect pollen-bearing bees and to research the relationship between the environmental factors and pollen-bearing bee rate. For this purpose, authors use YOLOv3-tiny object detection model to detect bees followed by a Kalman filter used for their tracking. The authors also use tracked bee paths to determine if the bees are incoming or leaving and they can process the data with a frame rate of 25 FPS. Using the bee traffic and pollen-bearing data, authors compute several parameters describing bee colony activity. The overall F1-score for pollen and non-pollen bearing bee recognition is 0.94. Authors of the another study (2021) aim on counting bees entering and leaving the beehive using the YOLOv4 object detection. Proposed system uses a special beehive entrance consisting of parallel tunnels from acrylic glass equipped with a special valve, which ensures that bees move only in one direction. This entrance is captured by a camera placed above the tunnels. Individual bees are recognized using the YOLO model and tracked using the DeepSort model. Detection accuracy of the proposed approach is 99.5%. In the study (2022), the authors used their own videos of the bee traffic in front of a hive to create a bee tracking framework. It consists of a YOLOv5 object detector trained on the bees with and without the pollen used for the bee localization and StrongSort method with MobileNetV2 and OSNet networks for the bee tracking. This approach reaches a precision of 0.98 for detecting both bee classes in the detection part and ATA score of 0.8 for the tracking part. Nevertheless, ATA score significantly drops to 0.2 with an increase of the bee traffic and a higher amount of bees in the video. Inference times for single frames varied on the used hardware between 70–150 ms, which should be suitable for the real-time processing. Authors of (2022) intend to design a complex system for bee colony health state monitoring based on the automated honey bee recognition using a camera in front of an examined beehive and SSD object detector. At this moment, SSD is used only for bee counting and the camera frames are sent to the web application. Authors used their own dataset, which is not further described or made publicly available. In the proof-of-concept studies Sledeviv́ and Plonis (2023) and Sledevič and Abromavičius (2023), the authors focus on the bee activity monitoring on the beehive’s entrance. To achieve this, they use the YOLOv8m object detector for the localization and ByteTrack package for tracking of the individual bees. Detected bee paths and their speeds

text: 3.4. General bee inspection applications In the general bee inspection field, the experiments often focus on distinguishing between the bees and other insects. This could be particularly useful for example in the Asian giant hornet and other intruder’s detection, or bee anomalous appearance detection, where the results could lead to a fast response of the beekeeper and potential damage mitigation. This application field also covers the brood cell inspection field as in (2018), or bee grooming behavior investigation described in (2017), where machine learning and computer vision techniques might be very time-saving and easily used for the inspection and research purposes. The Fig. 9 shows how a generic application designed to determine the species (or a general nature) of a detection may be realized. 3.4.1. Conventional techniques based general bee inspection Several studies focus on the visual analysis of the brood cells. One of those studies compares several conventional classification techniques in order to recognize the brood cells uncapping and shows the possibility of such in-field measurements, even with the presence of bees. Another study covering the same topic is described in the following section focused on the CNN-based methods. In (2008), the authors apply a state-of-the-art algorithm in bee tracking and behavior prediction on the problem of bee dance analysis. They use a generic model of the insect consisting of three ellipses and use it to track movements of the bee in a video sequence. Further they design and implement a Markov chain based motion model that serves as a behavior model. For a particular dance, the waggle dance, they also estimated parameters of interest describing the dance. In the proof-of-concept study , the authors aim to classify bee subspecies using their own dataset of the insect wings. To achieve this, they use classical image processing techniques, handselected and pixel-based wing features and several classifiers as the LDA, k-NN, Logistic regression or SVM. The authors reached the highest classification accuracy of 84% and they compare the achieved results with the older studies. An interesting study using classical image processing techniques is presented in (2017), where the authors explore the bee grooming process by observing the bees covered with baking flour. For the grooming process analysis conventional computer vision methods were used, such as color corrections and binary segmentation. The authors of (2018) propose a conventional CV based method for counting brood from frame images called CombCount. The system implemented in Python OpenCV classifies and counts comb cells into categories such as uncapped, capped honey, capped brood and more, and segments the images accordingly. Not only is the count information provided, but also the distribution of the classes, allowing for further study. The authors provide data on newly formed colony brood and honey store development.

text: 3.4.2. CNN classifiers based general bee inspection In (2019) a cloud-based CNN model called DeepBees is introduced. The aim is to provide an integrated multi-task deep learning model for automating hive monitoring where all the modules utilize a common feature extractor. The individual tasks are represented by modules, such as the genus module (classification between honey bee, wasp, hornet and a bumblebees), the classification module (classifying pollen carrying worker bees, non-carrying worker bees, drones and dead bees), pollen module implementing an SSD based pollen detector, and a pose module estimating a bee’s pose via 32 key points. The system is now deployed and is being further developed. Another framework for the pose estimation is presented in (2022). The study (2020), similarly as (2007) focusing on the brood cells, extends the older one with deep learning techniques in order to detect seven possible states of the brood cell from a single image using common CNN classifiers and data augmentation techniques. The authors also provide their dataset and the analysis software. Authors of the study (2020b), likewise the (2014), classify the bee subpsecies based on the wings samples. For this task, they utilize the MobileNet classifier they introduce a whole collaborative system DeepABIS consisting of a mobile and a cloud based application. From the various versions of the MobileNet and DenseNet classifiers, the authors reached the best results with the MobileNet-PR-CW model with the resulting F1-score of 0.9. Authors also tested the adapted framework on an additional butterfly dataset leading to the accuracy of 96.7%. The framework is publicly available at (2020a). The research presented in Kaplan (2021) compares several CNN architectures on datasets presented in Schurischuster and Kampel (2020b), Yang (2018) and (2018a). The authors used the originally proposed classes of each dataset and they proved that the common CNN classifiers such as ResNet, AlexNet, or VGG are able to solve those tasks with a comparable accuracy to the original works, which used conventional classifiers or custom CNNs. The paper provides the inference times and performance analysis of all models used. In the paper De (2022), the authors aims to detect bee subspecies based on the bee wings as in the older studies (2014) and (2020b). The authors use several CNN models (ResNet-50, MobileNet V2, Inception Net V3 and Inception ResNet V2) to classify eight Western honey bee (Apis mellifera) subspecies based on the bee wing samples collected by the CREA-Research center for Agriculture and Environment. The best average accuracy of 0.99 was achieved with both Inception Net models. The authors also compared the achieved results with the previously used morphometric models, where the CNN based approach proved to be give from 7% up to 60% more accurate results among the examined samples. The author of Liang (2022) compares the performance of four CNN models (VGG19, InceptionV3, MobileNet and custom CNN architecture) together with an SVM classifier fed directly by the image data. The dataset used in this paper is taken from Yang (2018). A F1-score over

Object detection based general bee inspection The authors of Dembski and Szymański (2019) focus on the effect of the used color model on the accuracy of the selected object detector, which is probably a R-CNN based, but unfortunately not further specified. They use their own dataset consisting of a short videos of the beehive entrance and three color models: gray scale, RGB and HSV. The authors compare resulting ROC curves and they conclude, that the best results were achieved with the RGB model followed by the HSV. A high potential of the object detection approach for bee traffic monitoring is also shown in (2022). Authors of this paper use YOLOv5 network and their own dataset for the detection and recognition between the bee workers and drones from the traffic at the bee hive’s entrance. The detection average precision over both classes and all test datasets was 92%. The paper is unfortunately not published in English. 3.5. Commercially available options When investigating the beehive monitoring systems, we also found several commercially available solutions, and these are briefly introduced herein. While vision-based options exist, the market is still dominated by sensor systems, which usually exploit the scales, acoustic measurements, or other sensors. The vision-based systems are regularly placed at the beehive entrance; however, we also found a configuration based on capturing the honeycomb frames. A brief overview of the vision- and the sensor-based products is provided below.

The research team in ApiVcelar (2023) provide a variety of solutions; their central model is ApiScale, which allows for remote weight monitoring to inform the beekeeper about the current stock levels, feeding needs, or an impending honey harvest. This setup also includes a humidity and temperature sensor that operates from −20 ◦ C to 50 ◦ C. The other system marketed is ApiTracker, which also includes temperature and humidity sensing as well as damage and theft protection and is a small enough to fit into the hive’s frame. A similar system is offered by the company ; the product utilizes a weighting scale, with an additional small sensor situated near the reducer or between the frames to record audio tracks and to measure humidity and temperature changes. The data relating to the weight and the sound recordings are stored locally and, when possible, uploaded to a cloud server via a transmitter; the details are then sent to the user via a smartphone app where they can be visualized. A maximum of 5 scales and 10 small sensors per transmitter is recommended. The approach promoted by Beehivemonitoring (2023) has yielded a complex system in the form of kits, such as the Hive heart 3.0 for temperature, humidity, and sound monitoring, to detect the death of a queen or a swarm mood change; these occurrences may indicate the future formation of a new brood. The internal memory can last up to 2 months, and the data are transmitted either via Bluetooth directly into a smartphone or over a GSM module. Furthermore, the system offers a beekeeping hive scale product in various forms. Each of the hive scale kits is delivered with a rechargeable battery, which reportedly endures up to 5 years on a single charge, and supports a hive of up to 200 kg in weight. Additionally, the customers can choose from a variety of other products, such as the Bee counting module, which uses entrance restriction and light gates to count the incoming and leaving bees. Other vendors that offer similar products include BeeSage (2023), Arnia (2023), BeeGuard (2023), Solutionbee

text: The composition of the examined techniques over the last ten years is shown in Fig. 11. Apparently, the CNN-based classifiers were first used in 2016; one of the pioneering CNN-based classifiers, the successful AlexNet, had nevertheless been introduced in 2014. The conventional procedures, however, dominated the segment until 2018 (considering only two investigated publications in the year 2017). The first use of the object detection techniques examined herein dates back to the year 2019, with a dominance since 2021 (assuming the introduction of the first YOLO detector in 2015 and the SSD detector a year later). The impact of the investigated problems on the selected techniques and vice versa is shown in Fig. 12. Exploring this impact, we established that the conventional techniques are used almost at an equal ratio in all of the investigated problems. The CNN classifier techniques are mostly utilized for the general bee classification, together with the Varroa monitoring. Finally, it is worth noting that the majority of the object detection articles and papers focus on bee traffic monitoring. To define the role of the techniques that find use in solving the investigated problems, we can note that, apparently, a half of the pollen detection options are covered by conventional methods followed by CNN classifiers and complemented with a rather small impact of object detectors. A similar situation can be observed in the Varroa monitoring. Another issue lies in the bee traffic monitoring problem, where a half of the applications are solved by object detectors and conventional techniques; the impact of the CNN classifiers is, as in the previous case, rather small. In the general bee inspection, the majority of the problems are resolved by the CNN classifiers and conventional techniques.

doc_id: 094Machine learning and computer vision techniques in continuous beehive monitoring applications A survey.txt
text: Exploring the described research trends, we could identify a significant rise in the application of the object detector techniques, together with an increase in the number of articles and papers on bee traffic monitoring. In this context, we have formulated two hypotheses: One proposes that the pollen and Varroa mite detection-related problems are solved to a sufficient degree mostly by the state-of-the-art CNN classifiers (but partially also with the conventional techniques), meaning that the research moves on to the more complicated domain of bee traffic monitoring and motion pattern recognition; the other then assumes that the bee traffic monitoring problem is often solved in a rather complicated environment, such as the beehive entrance seen from above, with a large number of moving bees. This latter scenario would be problematic to solve due to the necessity of segmenting the individual objects, a task requiring conventional techniques (or additional CNN classifiers). In contrast, the performance of the stateof-the-art object detectors, including the SSD or YOLO, allows real-time processing on the embedded devices. Applications such as the Varroa mite monitoring and pollen detection could be simplified by setting a proper scene with a non-disturbing background and potential physical segmentation of the individual bees on the captured scene. It is then possible to assume that, under the presumption of an appropriate scene and simplifying the problem to multi-class classification, the CNN-based classifiers might be more suitable for the given tasks. If the current trend persists, meaning that the usage of conventional techniques continues to stagnate, the contribution of such techniques will probably slowly decline in favor of CNN-based classifiers and object detectors. Although the modern deep learning methods allow for an efficient solution of complex tasks, such as image and speech recognition and machine translation, a further increase of their accuracy may approach

doc_id: 094Machine learning and computer vision techniques in continuous beehive monitoring applications A survey.txt
text: the technical and economic limits of the state-of-the-art algorithms and computational hardware, as described in (2021). With this option in mind, despite the lower computational power of the embedded hardware (but, conversely, exploiting their usually better intelligibility), classic CV techniques might still embody a good alternative in many applications. Nevertheless, we believe that multiple older studies using the conventional approach might be significantly improved by using deep learning or object detection approaches, which also allows us to collect markedly more information from the given data if only a single model is utilized. 5. Possible trends in the future research In terms of the investigated application fields, the amount of published research output, and the trends in the automated beehive monitoring segment, we can observe a rising number of articles and papers focusing on the entire sector. The relevant applications cover a wide variety of provinces within pollen and Varroa mite detection, bee traffic monitoring, brood cell inspection, bee and other insect species recognition, and bee behavior investigation. Generally, there remains a significant obstacle to the deep learning approach, namely, a lack of a well-designed, annotated datasets at a high resolution. A viable approach to eliminate the issue could lie in joining in some pre-existing datasets; such a step is nevertheless potentially problematic due to the different environments. An alternative solution is to create a new complex dataset compiled through long-term in-field measurement containing sufficient amounts of bees in various states (pollen-bearing, Varroa mite infestation, and others), drones, or even bee queens. It could be interesting to also cover other sensor data, such as sound, in-hive temperature and humidity, and beehive weight. These procedures would not only allow developing complex deep learning-based systems usable in long-term bee colony monitoring but also recognizing potentially dangerous situations. A worthwhile opportunity for such systems rests in the broad range of available embedded devices, such as the Raspberry Pi family and the NVIDIA Jetson range. The equipment is widely used, relatively affordable, and offers multiple accessories, such as cameras and sensors. Regarding the Raspberry Pi, the computational power may be too low for an efficient training of neural networks but suffices in real-time image processing via selected pre-trained models. Together with the well-accessible 3D print technologies, this allows for rapid prototyping and delivers good customization possibilities for various applications. Finally, the discussed scenarios also prepare the ground for a more intensive involvement of the beekeeping community members, who previously might not have had good access to the widely available state-of-the-art beehive monitoring devices. Comparing the sensor and machine vision approaches, we assume that both are usable in a manner that will enable them to complement each other. Sensors such as microphones; weight scales; and CO2, temperature, and humidity detectors could provide satisfactory information about the overall situation in the beehive; the vision-based systems, however, facilitate investigating the individual bees; detecting pollen, Varroa mite infestation, or various deformations; and monitoring bee traffic or other insects, which would be complicated if not impossible by using only the sensor approach. Presumably, at present, the trends in the continuous beehive monitoring are leading to a higher fusion between the sensor- and visionbased systems. The commonly used sensors bring a high amount of information on the bee activity throughout the day and, in combination with a vision-based system detecting the pollen-bearing bees or the number of incoming and outcoming bees (and potentially also the amount of Varroa-infested bees or bee robbing), they may produce a complex system to provide for an early detection of urgent states, together with long-term bee colony statistics. All of the above factors could reduce the workload on the beekeepers, help to improve understanding the behavior of bees, and ensure for bees better living conditions without unnecessary interventions or untargeted treatments. To outline our future research, we can emphasize a focus on further development of the continuous bee health monitoring device that combines the visual capturing of bees with various sensor data, including details on the temperature, humidity, and CO2; the scheme is briefly described in Nevláčil et al. (2023) and available in (2023d). The device was employed to capture the dataset c) where we continuously cover three days in a beehive at one location; however, we would like to expand the time and extent to obtain at least one month of continuous monitoring on two beehives at two separate locations. Such data would then enable us to improve the Varroa mite detection system presented in (2021); the setup is based on the YOLOv5 object detector. Further, the same data carry a major potential to allow refining an experiment with the reconstructionbased anomaly detection approach described in (2023a); the procedure is trained on the OK data only and could facilitate detecting various anomalies without a need of their annotations. The ultimate goal is to develop the beehive monitoring device and the surrounding framework into a distributable platform that will be deployed and contributed to through the efforts of the beekeeping community.

text: Beekeeping and the Need for Pollination from an Agricultural Perspective in Egypt Yahya Al Naggar, Garry Codling, John P. Giesy and Anton Safer Introduction The natural environment and modern society are inextricably bound to pollinators; these pollinators maintain a healthy, genetically diverse ecosystem of wild plants and are essential in the pollination of many food crops and, thus, food security . Although some mammals, reptiles, and birds are pollinators, insects are by far the most common. Bees, including honey bees, bumble bees, and solitary bees, are the most prominent and economically important pollinators worldwide. Of all the bee species (>20,000 species in 9 families), the western honey bee (Apis mellifera) is by far the most commonly managed. The economic value of pollination in 2009, based on the global crop market, was estimated in several ways. The minimum estimate was US$164 billion (Gallai, Salles, Settele, & Vaissière, 2009), while the estimate ranged from US$235 to US$577 billion annually if assessed based on an enhancement of crop yields . The value of the western honey bee’s pollination in the USA alone has been estimated at some US$15 to US$20 billion annually (vanEngelsdorp, Hayes Jr, Underwood, & Pettis, 2010). In the USA, >90% of all almond, blueberry, and apple crops are reliant upon this one pollinator species . The vital role that pollinators play in enhancing the productivity of various crops, such as fruits and nuts, vegetables and pulses, oil and forage crops, have often been underestimated especially in developing countries, where pollinator services are considered to be more important because a greater proportion of the inhabitants is maintained by income from agriculture. Egypt is no exception. Crops that are fully dependent on pollinators, such as melons (including DOI: 10.1080/0005772X.2018.1484202 © 2018 International Bee Research Association

text: Beekeeping in Egypt It could be argued that, of all the countries of the ancient world, Egypt has the longest documented history of honey bee use. The founder of the First Dynasty of Egyptian Kings, King Menes, was known as the “beekeeper” (~4445 BC), and future rulers were often referred to as the “Bee King” . The oldest drawings and paintings on tombs, sarcophagi, temples, and obelisks in Egypt clearly show how beekeeping was an intrinsic part of society (Figure 1). Beekeeping in ancient Egypt was characterized by the use of cylindrical hives and production of huge amounts of honey (Figure 2). Modern beekeeping in Egypt began in 1918, when a group of amateurs began to use wooden Langstroth frames and formed the first association of beekeepers in 1920. Since then and continuing on up until today, this association has issued regular bulletins to improve upon methods and share ideas about beekeeping in Egypt

doc_id: 077Beekeeping and the Need for Pollination from an agricultural perspective in egypt.txt
text: Some crops do not necessarily depend on animal pollination, but largely benefit from animal pollination. These crops include three major ones that are widespread in Egypt: berseem which is known as Egyptian clover (Trifolium alexandrinum L.), cotton, and maize (Dixit, Singh, & Gupta, 1989). Also, the area devoted to fruits increased by almost 165% in 1989, and the areas dedicated to winter and summer vegetables increased by about 94% and 32%, respectively (Goueli & El Miniawy, 1994). The increase in area allocated to fruits and vegetables and the minimal effect of honey bee parasites and pathogens observed during this period were significant in that these factors correlated with the increasing numbers of beehives. The decline in numbers of beehives in Egypt observed between 1990 and 2016 could be attributed to several factors: (1) the destructive effects of the Varroa mite, which represents the major ectoparasite of honey bees in Egypt and worldwide and was first observed in Egypt in 1983 (Abou-Shaara & Tabikha, 2016); (2) the loss of agricultural land in Egypt due to urbanization (El-Hefnawi, 2005; El-Wakeil & Abdallah, 2012) that led to decreasing trends in cultivated crops that depend on honey bee pollination; (3) the extension and overuse of pesticides against different crop pests, which negatively affect bees (Al ; Codling, Al Naggar, Giesy, & Robertson, 2018); (4) adverse effects of in-hive chemicals used to protect and treat honey bees against pathogens and parasites; and/ or (5) the introduction of alien species (Shebl, Kamel, & Mahfouz, 2013). Besides the number of beehives per year and country, the FAO database reports also included figures on honey (Figure 3) and beeswax production (not shown here). We also calculated the honey yield per beehive (lowest curve of Figure 3). Egyptian honey production was on the rise from 1961 up until the mid-1980s, with a parallel growth of productivity from an average of 3 kg/hive to 10 kg. From the mid-1980s to the 1990s, a pronounced decline to 6 kg/hive was observed and, later, a slow decline to 5–6 kg/hive stabilized productivity.

text: Article There are numerous ways a grower, with little or no intimate knowledge of the life and habits of pollinating animals, can assess the effectiveness of the pollination of his or her crop by observing the compact clusters of fruits or seeds, and uniform set. For example, adequate pollination is indicated by the presence of two or more muskmelons near the crown or base of the vine, or a majority of the apples developing from the king, or primary bud or bloom, at the tip of the cluster. In a watermelon field, adequate pollination would be indicated by a high percentage of melons in the number 1 class, that is, symmetrical, completely developed throughout, and of satisfactory weight . In Egypt, the effects of bee pollination that increase the yields, vitamin content, and quality of different crops have been extensively investigated (Blal, Kamel, Mahfouz, & El-Wahed, 2012; Bondok, El Nahrawy, & Esa, 2016; Taha & Bayoumi, 2009). For example, the foraging behavior of honey and wild bees and their effects as pollinators have been evaluated on summer seed watermelon flowers in Kafr El Sheikh Governorate, Egypt, in 2006 . The authors of this study recorded 11 insect species as pollinators on summer seed watermelon plants, and A. mellifera was the predominant species seen. Their results also revealed that one hectare of summer seed watermelon could produce 10 kg of honey per season. Open pollination treatment produced the highest number of mature fruits and seed yield compared to caged plants lacking insect visitors, which did not produce any fruits.

Although the trend varied in the 1980s with rates above 2, a consistent, overall, downward trend was observed from 1990 up until 2016, the last year of data included in the FAO database. After 1990, as the number of beehives dwindled, the rate decreased constantly to less than 1 hive per ha. This is a clear evidence for the immediate current threat to pollination in Egypt (Figure 5) as this rate is expected to deteriorate in future if it is not counteracted. text: Conclusions Since these are vital for the agricultural productivity of Egypt, the benefits of and threats to populations of both managed honey bees and wild pollinators should be major considerations. Due to a pronounced loss in the number of beehives and a decline of productivity, Egypt produced only 40% of the honey production in 2016 (5,000 tons) that it produced at its peak period in 1987 (12,500 tons) (Figure 3). This justifies going on red alert, as declines in both hive numbers and productivity are markers of forced loss of pollinators. There are three complementary approaches that can be taken to safeguard pollinators in agro-ecosystems (ecological intensification, strengthening diversified farming systems, and ecological infrastructure) , and these should be taken into consideration. Maintaining biodiversity is an important key to improve the resilience of pollinators and guarding against deficiencies in pollination. Relying not only on honey bees as the dominant pollinator species in Egypt, but also protecting the less abundant and rarer species, as “each farmer requires his or her crops to be adequately pollinated” . Pesticide use can do more harm to this goal than has been perceived by farmers up until now. Moreover, a program for scientific investigation should be launched as soon as possible to fulfill the 2030 Sustainable Development Agenda.

doc_id: 088Beekeeping and Managed Bee Diversity in Indonesia Perspective and Preference of Beekeepers.txt
text: 1. Introduction Bees provide invaluable ecosystem services in Asia especially Indonesia such as pollination services and their production of honey, brood, propolis, wax, bee pollen, royal jelly, and bee venom . Eusocial bees are diverse in Indonesia and encompass three groups, i.e., honey bees, stingless bees, and bumble bees, each of which stores resources such as honey in their nests . The high richness of species and uniqueness of the distribution of eusocial bees in Indonesia may relate to the country’s broad geographic expanse, varied topological and environmental landscape, and complex geological history . Indonesia has recorded five species of native honey bees , at least 46 species of stingless bees, and two species of bumble bees . Some species are recorded in all the islands of Indonesia, and some species are not. For instance, a stingless bee species, Tetragonula laeviceps, is widely distributed and covering the whole of Indonesia, while bumble bees (Bombus spp.) are only found in the highlands of Java and Sumatera . A recent study in Jambi, Sumatera, found 39 species of bees, including both the honey bees and the stingless bees . The native people of Indonesia have used honey for a long time, hunting for honey from both wild honey bees and stingless bees. Although regional beekeeping has been developed from traditional to modern hives and methods, traditional beekeeping remains in practice using local honey bees and stingless bees. Based on , national honey production reached 51,338 L in 2020, with the highest production in Java island amounting to 41,614 L (81.06%). While data for national honey production are not recorded properly, scattered evidence demonstrates that honey production by hunting Apis dorsata has taken a large role in national honey production . However, production from Apis mellifera in Indonesia has tended to decrease owing to changing food sources, pests, and climate change . In the last decade, rearing of stingless bees has gradually been developed and has resulted in the increase production of medicinal honey, propolis, and their derivative products. Indonesia needs to develop beekeeping by enhancing both existing natural ecosystems and semi-natural habitats as sources of food and also promoting native stingless bees . This paper investigates beekeeping practices and provides updates on managed bee diversity in Indonesia. The result will be a very valuable information for bee conservation as well as to achieve sustainable honey production. The challenges for beekeeping include how to improve bee protection from the unpredictable environment and how to increase the quality of life and income of beekeepers . Global declines in honey bees and wild bees have been linked to pathogens, climate change, habitat fragmentation, and pesticide application . The potential threat from insecticides such as neonicotinoid to flowering crops has been the subject of considerable debate . Neonicotinoids have been shown to increase mortality in honey bees by impairing their homing ability and reducing the reproductive success of bumble bees and solitary bees . The short-term exposure to imidacloprid and ethion adversely affects honey bee foragers, and chronic exposure to glyphosate may affect pollination success , although other studies have identified no effects . There is limited information from replicated studies on the longer-term survival of honey bee colonies following exposure . Landscape-scale experiments under real world agricultural conditions are needed to integrate spatial, temporal, and species-specific variation in order to understand the impacts of neonicotinoids on bees . The colony collapse disorder (CCD) phenomena was recorded for the first time from the survey of beekeepers in USA, and it was then extensively monitored in USA from 2006 to 2016 . It was also followed by researchers in China from 2013 to 2017 and other countries . FAO also conducted a global survey of honey bees and pollinators around the world. In Indonesia, there is a lack of data on CCD and other data pertaining to bee population, managed bee diversity and beekeepers’ practices. This research was conducted to study the bee diversity that are being managed by beekeepers across Indonesia and to investigate the beekeepers’ perspectives on the factors that affect bee populations and their products. Previous research found that the population of honey bees is affected by habitat types and that beekeepers prefer to put their hives isolated from agricultural areas . There was indication of the population decline of A. cerana in East Java, although the result was not conclusive. More research is needed to understand whether population decline of honey bees is happening in Indonesia. This research was also aimed to understand the beekeepers’ perspectives on factors affecting bee populations and their product such as pollen and beeswax. Studies in Europe detected pesticide residue in honey bees products from Spain , Germany , Greece , and France , while in Indonesia, traces of residues has been detected, albeit at very low concentrations . 2. Materials and Methods 2.1. Selected Area for Survey Research Based on our previous research in Java (see ), beekeepers have recently kept not only honey bees but also stingless bees for their honey and propolis production. Therefore, the survey research was conducted in the center of honey bee or stingless bees production in different areas in Indonesia. We initiated the inventory of beekeepers or honey bees or stingless bees’ production area in Indonesia by taking into consideration the geographical representative which include Sumatera, Java, Kalimantan, Sulawesi, Bali, Nusa Tenggara, and Maluku (Figure 1, Table 1). The beekeepers were selected based on representation of each province in Indonesia. The number of samples were chosen based on proportion of beekeepers in each geographical area.

doc_id: 088Beekeeping and Managed Bee Diversity in Indonesia Perspective and Preference of Beekeepers.txt
text: 4. Discussion Based on our research, there is a wide variation in ages, experiences, business scales, and educational background of the beekeepers in Indonesia. It is interesting to note that it seems that there is no relationship between the educational background of beekeepers and the number of hives or business scale of beekeeping. Based on the interview, it seems that over time, the small-scale beekeeping tends to increase the numbers of their boxes or hives, while the large scale tends to stay constant. This indicates that the beekeeping business is promising at a large scale. Although education is an important factor for economic growth, there is strong evidence that cognitive skills are more important and complementary to the quality of economic institutions . To run a successful beekeeping business, two sets of skills are needed, i.e., skills and experiences in beekeeping and skills/experiences the business itself. mentioned that the working world is changing rapidly and that people need to be equipped with the skills to unlearn and relearn at every stage of their lives, and they need the ability to remain flexible, adaptive, up to date with workplace requirements, and resilient. However, beekeeping is still considered to be the second class of farming in Indonesia, and therefore the beekeeping sector is still small. The national production of honey is considered part of forest production as a non-timber forest product . In addition, the honey production in Indonesia is still low and has not met the domestic demand for honey. For instance, estimated that honey production in the region of Kalimantan based on beekeeping with honey boards was between 53 kg and 267 kg per beekeeping family per year. It is estimated that domestic demand of honey needs 3750 ton per year, while the supply of honey is only 500–2000 ton per year . According to the data on honey import and export, in 2018, Indonesia imported honey and sugar much higher than it exported, with USD 265 million worth of export sugar and honey but an import value is USD 2126 million . Thus, this is a big opportunity for beekeeping business in Indonesia. As one of the global hotspots of autochthonous Apis bee diversity, Indonesia faces an incoming threat from the western honey bee, A. mellifera. The local bees, such as the Asian honey bee A. cerana, have tended to decline due to competition with A. mellifera . Although in this survey, the native honey bee, A. cerana is still the dominant species and is still managed by the beekeepers in Indonesia, in the near future, the biggest challenge for Indonesian honey production is to balance the beekeeping between A. mellifera and A. cerana. Our survey also found that beekeepers in different islands of Indonesia have different preferences in beekeeping, especially in choosing the bee species. For instance, beekeepers in Java mostly prefer to rear A. mellifera and A. cerana. This is arguably due to the fact that in Java has many plantations and wild plants that flower in different places and times that are suitable for the moving beekeeping system, especially A. mellifera . Many plants flower throughout the year, such as coconut (Cocos nucifera) and calliandra (Calliandra calothyrsus). Java formerly had a flowering calendar that gave guidance for the movement of A. mellifera, but it is no longer accurate because of the impacts of climate change . Our survey did not cover all bees in Indonesia, which have been reported to include five species of native honey bees and more than 46 species of stingless bees . The most common Apis species in Indonesia is A. cerana, which is characterized by a smaller body size compared to the other two species of domesticated honey bees, A. nigrocincta and A. koschevnikovi . This result is the same with the research by , who found A. cerana to be widespread on almost all islands in Indonesia. However, the populations of A. cerana in Indonesia are usually recognized as the subspecies A. cerana indica, which is distinct from other subspecies such as A. cerana javana . The subspecies A. cerana are known to vary across Indonesia, mostly related with latitude and elevation . Beekeepers in Java, Sumatera, Kalimantan, Sulawesi, and Bali still conduct traditional beekeeping of A. cerana in simple wooden boxes without frames or in the cavity of tree trunks . They are also familiar with traditional stingless bee keeping methods that utilize the cavities of cut tree trunks, bamboo, or simple wooden boxes as the colonies nest and place the nests around houses to make it easy to manage and harvest. As the most dominant stingless bee in this survey, T. laeviceps also are farmed in human settlements of many places in Indonesia. T. laeviceps and other native stingless bee species also tend to be managed in the areas which are adjacent of forests in Indonesia so that the forest can be conserved as well as managed stingless bees . Many studies reported showed the colony losses of honey bees with ranges 8–50% . In contrast, reported that 42% of monitored countries (27 countries) showed an increasing trend in genetic diversity of the honey bee population. Similarly to , the number of hives that were kept by beekeepers in Indonesia tend to increase significantly. It showed that the business of beekeepers tends to develop by increasing the numbers of bee colonies, particularly of stingless bees. However, their operation size is still smaller

Some flowers produce nectar only during early hours of the morning or late in the evening, dictating the pattern of hive weight variation. High wind speeds and rain disrupt bee activity and foraging. However rain can result in an increase in the weight of a hive because rain water can accumulate on the top of flat hive surface, and the wooden structure of the hive can absorb moisture resulting in weight gain. The effect of rain depends on the absence/presence/quality of paint on the outer-surface of wooden hives. Similarly, exposure to the sun or hot and dry weather can also lead to beehive structure losing moisture and weight. On the other hand, hives made of plastic or polystyrene cannot absorb any moisture, thus contributing very little to the hive weight variations. The design and structure of a beehive weighing scale also contribute to weight variations. The load sensors, Analog to Digital Converters (ADC), and the physical structure of the weighing scale are exposed to variations in temperature, humidity, and other environmental factors, which impact their performance. Research is continuing on better designs to improve the performance of beehive weighing scales . Many commercial beehive monitoring systems are also competing with each other to provide affordable weighing scales. However, given the durability and accuracy requirements of design, the cost of commercial beehive weighing scales is still high for a majority of beekeepers, preventing their large-scale deployment. Authors in conduct a very thorough survey of Precision Beekeeping. It is a branch of Precision Agriculture focused on management of apiaries based on the monitoring of individual bee colonies in order to minimize resource consumption, and maximize bee productivity. The authors in their bibliometric analysis discover that from a total of 73 cited papers which use data for training machine models, only 6 use weight of the hives. From our experience, this is because collecting weight data from hives even for research purposes is expensive and very challenging. The authors in their conclusion state that ‘‘Looking at beekeepers’ needs, it is clear that more affordable commercial solutions are to be developed’’. Given the high costs of weighing scales in beehive monitoring systems and the difficulties associated with their deployment, any progress towards cost-effective alternatives for weight measurements of hive will contribute significantly towards wide scale deployment of beehive monitoring systems. Deep learning models have been used successfully in predicting complex time series data . One of the recent works on swarm detection uses Deep Recurrent Autoencoders to detect the anomalies in beehive data. Swarming is an event where the colony’s queen leaves the hive with a part of the bee population from an existing colony to start a new colony. Authors in this study collected data from multiple hives using multiple temperature sensors per hive, along with weight, humidity and carbon dioxide sensors. Using Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) based Autoencoders, authors tested different sensor configurations, and also investigated the impact of sensor placement on swarm detection. The authors conclude that Autoencoders can be used to detect other anomalies in the hive as well. Authors in proposed the use of transformers, a deep network consisting of encoders and decoders with multihead attention for prediction the next correct word in machine translation. Transformers have since been used for prediction in machine vision , bio-informatics and many other areas. For the task of prediction, authors in compared different LSTM based networks on time series data. Six different models were adapted for the task of forecasting hourly rainfall, using the historical weather data of two decades from five different cities in the United Kingdom. The results showed that Bidirectional-LSTM

The water proofing of weighing scales has been a continuous concern, and heavy rains often cause malfunctioning of scales. Sensor data where weighing scale(s) showed unrealistic variations due to rain was also discarded from the dataset. Apis-Prime is designed to estimate the weight variation pattern for an entire day, hence problems with the data for even a few hours on a given day make the data for the entire day unreliable. Attempts to use interpolation to fix the outliers or missing sensor data did not provide adequate results because of the complex nature of bee colonies. Also, the beehives need regular inspections to ensure the health of bees. The hives used in this study were inspected every fortnight for most of the year, and once a month during winter to make sure that the bees are healthy, and the queen is laying eggs. In each of these inspections, hive frames were pulled out one by one, with the hive open for up to 30 min. Occasionally frames were added/removed/swapped during these inspections, which led to a change in the weight of the hive. For these reasons, data from the days of hive inspections is also not included in the dataset. The variation in total days of data collected from each system in Table 2 is a result of different days of deployment, as well as a different number of data days discarded from the dataset for each system. The data is collected with an interval of 10 min, resulting in 144 data points per hive per day. Apis-Prime is designed using 48 data points per day, with an interval of 30 min between consecutive samples, which is adequate to capture important variations in the hive weight and other sensor data. The 144 data points collected each day are used to increase the quantity of the training data, by extracting 3 sets of 48 data points from each day in the training set. The activity of bees has a high correlation with the position of the sun. The position of the sun not only depends on the time of the day but also on the season. To a large extent, the seasons around the globe are determined by the latitude . This information is important for the neural networks to generate reasonable daily estimates of weight. The information about the time of data acquisition and the week number of the year is included in the dataset after cyclical encoding using sine/cosine transformation. The cyclic encoding allows the start and end of the day/year to have a similar representation, and avoid big discontinuities that can result with linear transformation. For example, the last week of December (week-52) and the first week of January (week-1) have very similar times for sunrise/sunset, and similar seasonal conditions for a specific geographic location.

text: model and providing detailed insights into the usefulness of sensors and features for such estimations. To achieve these objectives, we chose a network that overcomes the limitations of previous work using random forests and LSTM approaches. Random forests are limited in handling high-dimensional data and time-based dependencies, while LSTM cannot provide insights on sensor and feature contributions. Our solution uses a single network of self-attention encoders that simultaneously handles high-dimensional data and captures long-range dependencies. The self-attention layer weights provide information about the richness of input data, while using two encoders in parallel captures dependencies across time as well as different sensors features. This approach allows us to simultaneously evaluate the monitoring system design and estimate hive weight with superior performance compared to previous works. Fig. 2 shows the network architecture of Apis-Prime. The architecture of Apis-Prime is based on the encoder of transformer . Transformers composed of encoders and decoders along with a multi-head attention mechanism were first introduced for the task of machine language translation. ApisPrime uses only the encoder part of the transformer with some modifications to estimate the weight data, using the data from other sensors as input. Previous sections explain how the weight of a beehive is dependent on many different factors. Apis-Prime exploits these dependencies to estimate/predict a series of weight values based on time series data collected from internal hive sensors, weather, and other relevant information. Input to the network is a set of daily data collected from internal hive sensors such as temperature, humidity, atmospheric pressure, CO2 , acoustics, and vibrations. Information about the weather, the week of the year (seasonal information), the time of the day, and the size of the hive are also part of the input. Self-attention layer looks for dependencies within (self) the input data to identify what parts should be paid more attention to. This helps make most of the input data which is rich with information. The network is trained to generate the daily weight variations (per frame) of a hive as an output, the labels of which are obtained using custom built weighing scales deployed with each sensor system. All the inputs are normalized to create a feature matrix F of size 48 × 36, where 48 is the number of data samples per day and 36 is the number of features (see Table 1) extracted from different sensors within each data sample. In Table 3, we provide the parameter settings used for the network. While several configurations of these parameters were tested, in this work we only report the ones that demonstrated optimal performance. We use the weight of a hive at midnight as a reference, and any change in hive weight between midnight and a time instance in next 24 Hr window is dependent upon all the bee activity and environmental conditions till that time instance. The time-series data/features from all the sensors for an entire day (midnight to midnight) are shaped into a 2-dimensional matrix, with features on one axis and time on the other. Self-attention encoders use this 2-dimensional matrix as input, and exploit the relationships and dependencies within this data. A discussed earlier, self-attention encoders and decoders were originally proposed for the task of language translation . The dependencies and relationships within a sentence exist only in a single dimension, as words in a sentence appear in a sequence one after the other. Hence, self-attention encoders by design pay attention to data in one dimension. However in case of multi-sensory data from beehives, the relationships exist in two dimensions; one across different sensors and the other across time. To exploit the relationships in both these dimensions, Apis-Prime uses two selfattention encoders in parallel, but with different inputs. EncoderA in Apis-Prime uses feature matrix (F) of entire day as input, and exploits the relationships between different features across sensor data. Whereas EncoderB uses the transpose of feature matrix

The second category is where the accumulated error for all 48 estimations within a day leads to an under-estimate or over-estimate of weight variation, as shown in examples of Fig. 4 (b) and (d) respectively. doc_id: 103Forest melliferous resources as a sustainable development factor of beekeeping.txt
text: Forest melliferous resources as a sustainable development factor of beekeeping Rida Sultanova*, Ildar I. Gabitov, Yulai A. Yanbaev, Fitrat G. Yumaguzhin, Maria V. Martynova, Ivan V. Chudov and Varys R. Tuktarov Bashkir State Agrarian University, 50-letiya Octyabrya St., 34, Ufa, Russian Federation, 450001 Abstract The management of beekeeping on forest lands is a vivid manifestation of the multifunctional use of forests, which is based on its target cultivation. The work shows the development of beekeeping, identifies factors affecting the sustainable development of this industry, sets priorities for increasing the efficiency of utilization of forest honey resources of the Southern Urals, including the main melliferous - Tilia cordata Mill. The nature and characteristics of the influence of weather and climatic factors on the growth of bee colonies, their physiological state, composition, age representation of natural melliferous woody plants, the onset dates and the duration of their flowering were determined. It was found that, of the silvicultural and inventory indicators, the composition and age, density and type of forest most strongly influence the yield of honey. The activities of forest care are close to them in terms of importance. A system of organizational measures has been proposed, through which high efficiency of using forest feed resources and sustainable development of beekeeping can be achieved: keeping an optimal number of bee colonies in an apiary - up to 150 hives, based on providing one bee colony of 50-60 thousand individuals with at least 130 kg of nectar; the location of apiaries in the 3-kilometer zone of growth of forest melliferous plants, taking into account the productive emergence of bees in the 2.5-3.0 km; establishing clear nomadic routes based on a geobotanic inventory of forest and agricultural melliferous plants. Increasing the target indicator - the nectar productivity of forests without a gap in their use both in space and in time - can be achieved by growing multi-tiered forests of different age from Tilia cordata Mill. Keywords Climatic conditions; flowering intensity; forest use; marketable honey; nectar production; Tilia cordata Mill

text: Introduction Modern trends in forestry development make provisions for the multipurpose forest use with beekeeping being one of its directions (Dokukin and Prokofiev, 2011). Specificity of the beekeeping industry has an inevitable effect on the forest use: it depends on the natural and climatic conditions, work seasonality, and special means of production such as land, forest resources, plants and animals. Kuryakov and Gayduchenko (2012) emphasized the need to intensify the beekeeping industry by improving the feeding base quality, in the conditions of a catastrophic reduction in the number of bee colonies in many countries of the world in recent years. This fact is confirmed by M.D. (2013) when studying the morphometric characteristics of working bees. In many cases low resistance to fungal and viral diseases according to M. Spivak (2008) causes the bee death. At the same time R.A. Ilyasov, M.N. Kosarev and F.G. Yumaguzhin (2014) found that under conditions of bee subspecies mass hybridization and loss of the dark forest gene pool (Central European) bees in most European countries, that Russia has a significant reserve of purebred populations of the dark forest bee Apis mellifera mellifera. These bees are characterized by high winter hardiness, ability to active and short-term honey collection. While the most widespread subspecies of Apis mellifera mellifera is the Burzian honeybee population, which is resistant to invasive diseases and aggressiveness. It is an important

doc_id: 103Forest melliferous resources as a sustainable development factor of beekeeping.txt
text: f­ actor for preventing the spread of infections and preserving the population. This population is locally preserved in the Southern Ural mountain forest zone on the territory of the Republic of Bashkortostan in Shulgan-Tash State Nature Reserve, Altyn Solok Nature Reserve and Bashkiria National Park. Despite the fact that “bortnichestvo” (beekeeping) is a historically developed unique Bashkir practice, today 99% of the Burzyan population Apis mellifera mellifera bee colonies are found in beehives with frame hives and only about 1% lives in natural and artificial hollows in tree trunks – wild hives and decks. Apis mellifera mellifera According to the records extrapolation 200–400 “dichkov” (wild bees) which are monomorphic with bees kept in apiaries in this zone live in natural hollows. Although according to the research by Torné-Noguera (2014), the number of wild bees near the apiary is significantly reduced because of the potential impact of beekeeping bees on a wild bee. Geneticists studies have revealed the purity of the gene pool of the Burzyan Wild-Hive Honeybee population and its belonging to the subspecies A. m. mellifera. However, the genetic characteristics of native honeybees make it extremely vulnerable to undesirable changes in the gene pool of the population. Uncontrolled importation of other breeds and populations bees into the Burzyan Wild-Hive Honeybee area can lead to their crossbreeding. Cases of hybridization with southern breeds were revealed by Yumaguzhin and Yangbaev (2014), when analyzing the belonging to the Central Russian breed with the help of morphometric and molecular genetic analysis of the intergenic locus variability. In this connection, the population of the Burzyan honey bee must constitute a local genotypic reservoir, and needs to be preserved in the natural habitat in the mountain forest zone of the Southern Urals in the forests of Tilia cordata Mill, where it is believed their evolutionary development took place. In the Russian Federation, the total area of forests from honey woody plants such as Tilia cordata Mill, Acer platanoides L., Salix caprea, is 4,886 thousand ha, of which 67% are occupied by Tilia cordata Mill plantations. The ­Republic of Bashkortostan is dominated by T. cordata. The age of forests predominated with honey plants is extremely uneven and is composed of mature, and over-­ mature stands. Tilia cordata Mill occupies 1.14 million hectares out of the 5.7 million hectares of the forest fund of the Republic of Bashkortostan, mature and overgrowth – 637.1 thousand ha (55.9%), young growth – 72.2 thousand ha (6.3%), with an average age of lime plants – 61.1 years. This fact has both positive and negative sides. The ripening and mature plantations are the best for nectar productivity: the one flower nectar yield of 50-year-old Tilia cordata Mill is 0,944 ± 0,351 mg, from the flower of abundantly flowering 100-year-old trees is up to 1,402 ± 0,112 mg. However, with increasing age, the nectar release ability decreases. In addition, the tendency to reduce young growth, both first and second classes of age, suggests a possible loss of this honey. According to the research of Koudegnan (2012, 2015) the majority of plants out of 45 species currently threatened with extinction in the Guinean zone of Togo are nectariferous plants. They are necessary for bees to produce honey. In the author’s opinion, the threat of plants extinction has the prospect of irreversible consequences not only for the plant environment, but for the entire ecosystem of living organisms. Reduction of forest area from Tilia cordata Mill from 100 to 30 thousand ha in the Chuvash Republic led to a sharp decline in honey collection so the average Republican yield of marketable honey per family of bees over the last twenty years decreased by an average of 4 kg . The need for the formation of the young growth is quite obvious, and the development of measures taking into account the multipurpose of forests use from Tilia cordata Mill is a priority. The scientific elaboration of the use of forests as a feeding base for beekeeping is at an early stage of development, based mainly on monographic studies. In general, scientific publications are devoted to the increase of forest woody and ecological productivity, determined by its biomass or protective properties, ability to compensate for technogenic, recreational and other loads. Despite the fact that the Russian Federation fully provides for the need for honey from its own production, and since 2014 it has become a net exporter of honey, occupying the 39th place among Taiwan and Serbia among others, the

doc_id: 103Forest melliferous resources as a sustainable development factor of beekeeping.txt
text: forest is often regarded only as a source of wood , and not as a natural resource for obtaining food for beekeeping. Selection of the beekeeping direction in the forest use is a clear expression of the forestry science differentiation, which is based on the forestry functional and target model. The system of measures developed in the course of our studies is aimed at the predictable effect of increasing the forest use efficiency as a feeding base for beekeeping. When studying the problem of the forest use efficiency in beekeeping, long-term comprehensive studies to assess the performance of the beekeeping industry and to develop ways to increase the nectar productivity of natural forest melliferous lands were conducted. Four hypotheses have been put forward. From climatic factors the hydrothermal coefficient (HTC) and the amount of precipitation (1) have the greatest influence to the beekeeping indicators; the yield of marketable honey depends on the forestry and taxation indicators such as the breed composition, age, completeness and type of forest (2). The third hypothesis (3) is variability of Tilia cordata Mill trees according to the flowering intensity, interrelation of the tree flowering and biometric indices. The third hypothesis was of special importance in the studies. Differentiation of Tilia cordata Mill trees in bloom intensity allowed to put forward the fourth hypothesis (4) namely the concept of targeted forest management, where plantings around beekeeping apiaries are not formed abstractly, but with the help of forestry requirements, subordinated to one of the many functions of the forest – to provide beekeeping with forest melliferous resources. This concept involves the early formation of forests for specific purposes with strictly individual technology of creation and care. Incompatibility of the forestry and beekeeping purposes is the only limitation.

text: Materials and methods Objects of research The studies were conducted in the pure and mixed composition, single and uneven forests with Tilia cordata Mill in different natural zones of the Southern Urals in the ­Republic of Bashkortostan: the forests of the South Ural forest-steppe region; forest-steppe region and of ­coniferous-broad-leaved (mixed) forests the area of the European part of the Russian Federation. Only a certain part of potential forest Fund is involved in beekeeping activity. Forests actively used by bees within a radius of 3 km from stationary apiaries (determined by the area of productive flight of bees) were investigated. Along with them forests located near settlements and other protective forests are considered as a feeding base. Thus, melliferous resources include forests, close to the bee apiaries zone, and forests used by the nomadic form of beekeeping. In this case, attention is paid to forests species composition, dominated by coniferous stands (70.2%), and by deciduous such trees as Betula pendula, Populus tremula L., Alnus incana and Quercus robur L. (16,4%). Forests of these tree species do not perform a great melliferous role. However, their undergrowth storey namely Cerasus fruticosa (Pall), Padus racemosa (. Lam) Gilib, Viburnum opulus L., Rosa acicularis Lindl,
